MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Harold Jaffe of the law offices of Harold Jaffe (510-452-2610) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Stephen P. Ellingson of the law offices of Hayes Scott Bonino Ellingson & McLay LLP (650-637-9100) appears as counsel for defendants/moving parties

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTS DEREK SENE and STATE FARM GENERAL INSURANCE COMPANY's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings is GRANTED without leave to amend. State Farm's request for judicial notice is GRANTED. The judicially noticeable documents conclusively show that the doctrines of res judicata and/or collateral estoppel bar all claims asserted by plaintiffs Charles Harb and the Chrels Harb Revocable Living Trust. The same primary right is at issue in both cases, i.e., Mr. Sene's failure to provide the requested insurance. (Weikel v. TCW Realty Fund II Holding Co. (1997) 55 Cal.App.4th 1234.) In Harb I (Harb v. Sene, CGC-12-523951), the Court of Appeal expressly rejected the contention that the statute of limitations did not accrue until 2013 when Mr. Harb learned that State Farm could not sell the insurance that he requested. (Decision at 8-9.) Mr. Harb is now precluded from re-litigating that issue. Because plaintiffs' claims accrued at the latest in 2009, this case, which was filed on December 15, 2016, is time barred. (Decision at 5, fn. 3; CCP 338.) Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Mitch L. Lyon, CSR No. 6840, e-mail: mlyon@sftc.org. (L&M302)

Defendants Derek Sene, State Farm General Insurance Company'S Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiffs' Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Aug-03-2017 Text Ruling

Defendants Derek Sene, State Farm General Insurance Company'S Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiffs' Complaint
Off Calendar Aug-03-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendants Derek Sene, State Farm General Insurance Company'S Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiffs' Complaint is DENIED. Plaintiffs' and Defendants' unopposed requests for judicial notice are granted. Defendants failed to meet their burden of proof that Plaintiffs' complaint is based on petitioning activity and Defendants therefore fail to meet prong one of the anti-SLAPP statute contained in CCP 425.16 (a). In City of Cotati v. Cashman (2002) 29 Cal.4th 69, the California Supreme Court explained: "[T]he statutory phrase 'cause of action ... arising from' means simply that the defendant's act underlying the plaintiff's cause of action must itself have been an act in furtherance of the right of petition or free speech. [Citation.] In the anti-SLAPP context, the critical point is whether the plaintiff's cause of action was based on an act in furtherance of the defendant's right of petition or free speech. [Citations.] 'A defendant meets this burden by demonstrating that the act underlying the plaintiff's cause fits one of the categories spelled out in section 425.16, subdivision (e)....' " (Id. at p. 78.) "In deciding whether the 'arising from' requirement is met, a court considers 'the pleadings, and supporting and opposing affidavits stating the facts upon which the liability or defense is based.' " (Cotati, supra, 29 Cal.4th at p. 79.) In this case, Plaintiffs' complaint is based on Defendants' alleged concealment that Mr. Sene could not provide Mr. Harb with the insurance against demolition that he had requested. This concealment started in 2005, which was years before the prior litigation, and the reference to the prior lawsuit (i.e. the alleged protected activity) is merely incidental to Plaintiffs' claim. (See Martinez v. Metabolife Intl, Inc. (2003) 113 Cal.App.4th 181, 188 ("a defendant in an ordinary private dispute cannot take advantage of the anti-SLAPP statute simply because the complaint contains some references to speech or petitioning activity by the defendant "); Peregrine Funding, Inc. v. Sheppard Mullin Richter & Hampton LLP (2005) 133 Cal.App.4th 658, 672-673 (2005).) Plaintiffs' reference to the prior case is background information and merely shows when Plaintiffs allegedly discovered the claim. The gravamen or principal thrust of Plaintiffs' claims is the alleged concealment and suppression of Defendants' business practice of not providing the type of insurance Plaintiffs had requested years before the lawsuit, not the statements made during Mr. Sene's deposition. (See Beach v. Harco National Ins. Co. (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 82 (insurer's bad faith conduct in handling uninsured motorist claim and arbitration does not implicate first prong of anti-SLAPP statute). The Court emphasizes that since the first prong is not met, it is unable to reach, and does not reach, the required second prong, namely, whether Plaintiffs have established a probability that they will prevail on the claims. In this regard, Defendants' assertions that Plaintiffs are attempting to re-litigate a prior action between the same parties that resulted in a binding judgment against Plaintiffs cannot be considered at this time by the Court in this anti-SLAPP motion since, because the first prong of the anti-SLAPP standard has not been met, the Court is prevented from reaching that issue. The denial of this motion is without prejudice to Defendants filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate motion on the issues of res judicata, collateral estoppel, and/or the statute of limitations. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Angela Bradstreet; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Not Reported. (D302)
